scenario_title: Delete an RRset while adding to that same RRset in one UPDATE
test_rationale: This scenario isolates the underspecified case where one UPDATE message first removes an RRset and then recreates the same RRset with a different A record. Because RFC 2136 does not say whether the server must process these operations strictly in order, treat them as a composite replace, or reject the combination, implementations can legitimately diverge. The follow-up QUERY on the same name/type makes the difference observable by checking whether y.campus.edu. A exists and, if it does, which address is retained.

group_1.behavior: Applies the delete_rrset and then the add_rr, resulting in the new A RRset being present after a NOERROR UPDATE.

group_2.behavior: Treats the mixed delete/add to the same name/type as a conflicting update and rejects it, or otherwise leaves the RRset absent after processing.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3.4.2 defines delete_rrset and add_rr as individual update actions, but it does not normatively specify how a server must resolve a single UPDATE that contains both a delete_rrset and an add_rr for the same owner name and type. RFC 2181 §5.2 emphasizes RRset atomicity, but it does not prescribe intra-message ordering semantics for contradictory edits. As a result, implementations may differ on whether the operations are applied sequentially, coalesced as a replace, or rejected as a semantic conflict, producing different reply codes and/or post-state.
